crops.name: Tomato

forest.notes: Dry season planting (Jan) avoids heavy fungal disease pressure from rains; nursery first then transplant

savanna.notes: Dry season cultivation near water sources is common and reduces disease pressure

sahel.notes: Irrigated dry-season tomato is a major cash crop here

soil.type: Rich, well-drained loamy soil with good organic matter
soil.testing_tip: Tomatoes are heavy feeders - if the soil looks pale/sandy with no dark organic matter, mix in compost or manure generously before planting

fertilizer.basal: Compost or manure mixed into planting holes, plus NPK 15:15:15 at transplanting
fertilizer.topdress: NPK or calcium nitrate every 2-3 weeks during fruiting to prevent blossom-end rot
fertilizer.organic_alternative: Well-rotted manure plus wood ash for potassium, which improves fruit quality

watering.frequency: Water every 2-3 days, more frequently in very hot/dry weather; avoid wetting leaves to reduce disease
watering.critical_stages: Flowering and fruit development - inconsistent watering causes blossom-end rot and fruit cracking

common_pests_diseases.name: Tomato leaf miner (Tuta absoluta)
common_pests_diseases.sign: Tunnels in leaves and holes in fruit
common_pests_diseases.remedy: Use pheromone traps, remove and destroy infested leaves, rotate crops

common_pests_diseases.name: Early/late blight
common_pests_diseases.sign: Dark spots on leaves spreading to stems and fruit
common_pests_diseases.remedy: Avoid overhead watering, stake plants for airflow, remove infected leaves promptly

common_pests_diseases.name: Blossom-end rot
common_pests_diseases.sign: Dark sunken patches on fruit bottom
common_pests_diseases.remedy: Ensure consistent watering and adequate calcium in soil

harvest_timeline.signs_of_readiness: Fruit color changes from green to the variety's mature color (red, orange etc), slightly soft to touch
harvest_timeline.harvest_method: Hand-pick fruit with a slight twist, harvest every 2-3 days as fruits ripen at different times

beginner_tips: Start seeds in a nursery bed or seed tray, transplant seedlings after 3-4 weeks when they have 4-5 true leaves
Stake or trellis plants early - this keeps fruit off the ground and reduces rot and pest access
Dry season tomato (with irrigation) often has fewer diseases than rainy season tomato due to lower humidity

crops.name: Pepper (Bell/Chili)

forest.notes: Can be grown in dry season with irrigation, less disease pressure than wet season

savanna.notes: Dry season vegetable production is common near rivers/wells

soil.testing_tip: Similar requirements to tomato - rich soil with good drainage, avoid waterlogging which causes root rot

fertilizer.topdress: Light NPK application every 3-4 weeks during fruiting
fertilizer.organic_alternative: Manure-rich planting holes, mulching to retain moisture

watering.frequency: Water every 2-3 days, peppers don't like waterlogging but also don't tolerate prolonged drought
watering.critical_stages: Flowering through fruit set - water stress here causes flower drop

common_pests_diseases.name: Aphids/whiteflies
common_pests_diseases.sign: Curled leaves, sticky residue, sooty mold
common_pests_diseases.remedy: Wash with water spray, neem extract, encourage natural predators

common_pests_diseases.name: Anthracnose
common_pests_diseases.remedy: Avoid overhead watering, remove infected fruit promptly, ensure good spacing

common_pests_diseases.name: Bacterial wilt
common_pests_diseases.sign: Sudden wilting of whole plant despite moist soil
common_pests_diseases.remedy: Remove and destroy affected plants immediately, rotate crops

harvest_timeline.days_to_maturity: 70-90 days from transplanting for first harvest, continues for months
harvest_timeline.signs_of_readiness: Fruit reaches full size and changes color from green to red/yellow/orange depending on variety and ripeness desired
harvest_timeline.harvest_method: Hand-pick with a slight twist or use scissors, regular harvesting encourages more flowering

beginner_tips: Start in nursery beds like tomato, transplant after 4-5 weeks
Pepper plants can keep producing for several months if well cared for - regular harvesting is key
Green peppers are unripe fruit of the same plant - if you wait, they turn red/orange/yellow and become sweeter

crops.name: Okra

forest.notes: Grows well in both rainy seasons

sahel.notes: Tolerates heat well, suited to this zone with adequate water

soil.testing_tip: Okra is fairly tolerant - if your soil grows reasonable maize, it will grow okra

fertilizer.basal: NPK 15:15:15 at planting, around 50-100kg per hectare
fertilizer.topdress: Light nitrogen topdress 3-4 weeks after planting boosts leafy growth before flowering
fertilizer.organic_alternative: Compost mixed into planting rows works well

watering.frequency: Water every 2-3 days if no rain; okra is moderately drought tolerant once established
watering.critical_stages: Flowering and pod development - consistent moisture gives tender pods

common_pests_diseases.name: Aphids
common_pests_diseases.sign: Clusters on shoots and leaf undersides

common_pests_diseases.name: Pod borers
common_pests_diseases.remedy: Harvest pods young and regularly, remove damaged pods

common_pests_diseases.name: Leaf spot/blight
common_pests_diseases.remedy: Improve spacing for airflow, remove affected leaves

harvest_timeline.days_to_maturity: 50-65 days to first harvest, continues for weeks
harvest_timeline.signs_of_readiness: Pods are 5-10cm long, still tender (snap easily, not woody)
harvest_timeline.harvest_method: Cut pods with a knife every 2-3 days - frequent harvesting keeps the plant producing and pods tender

beginner_tips: Harvest pods young and often - if left too long they become tough and fibrous and the plant slows production
Thin to one strong seedling per hole after 2 weeks if you planted 2 seeds
Okra plants can grow quite tall (1.5-2m) - some farmers stake taller varieties

crops.name: Garden Egg (Eggplant/Aubergine)

forest.notes: Dry season cultivation common with irrigation

savanna.notes: Dry season vegetable, grown near water sources

soil.type: Fertile, well-drained sandy-loam soil rich in organic matter
soil.testing_tip: Similar to tomato and pepper - heavy feeder, needs organic matter worked in before planting

fertilizer.topdress: NPK application every 3-4 weeks during fruiting
fertilizer.organic_alternative: Generous compost application, mulching to retain moisture

watering.frequency: Water every 2-3 days, especially during fruiting

common_pests_diseases.name: Flea beetles
common_pests_diseases.remedy: Neem extract, remove weeds that host beetles nearby

common_pests_diseases.name: Fruit and shoot borer
common_pests_diseases.remedy: Prune and destroy affected shoots, harvest fruit promptly

harvest_timeline.days_to_maturity: 80-100 days from transplanting, continues for months
harvest_timeline.signs_of_readiness: Fruit reaches full size for the variety, skin glossy (not dull which indicates over-ripeness)
harvest_timeline.harvest_method: Cut fruit with a sharp knife leaving a short stem attached, harvest regularly to encourage more flowering

beginner_tips: Start in nursery, transplant after 4-5 weeks when seedlings have several true leaves
Regular harvesting (every few days) keeps plants productive for a long period
A reliable, low-maintenance crop once established - popular for both home consumption and local markets

crops.name: Onion

savanna.notes: Major dry season irrigated crop in middle belt and northern areas

sahel.notes: Onion is a key dry season cash crop here, grown with irrigation along rivers

soil.testing_tip: Onions need loose soil for bulb expansion - heavily compacted or clay soils should be well-tilled and amended with compost

fertilizer.topdress: Nitrogen topdress in early growth, switch to potassium-rich fertilizer as bulbs start forming
fertilizer.organic_alternative: Well-decomposed compost worked into beds before transplanting

watering.frequency: Regular watering every 2-3 days, reduce watering 2-3 weeks before harvest to help bulbs cure
watering.critical_stages: Bulb formation stage - inconsistent watering causes small or split bulbs

common_pests_diseases.name: Thrips
common_pests_diseases.sign: Silvery streaks on leaves, stunted growth
common_pests_diseases.remedy: Blue sticky traps, neem extract, avoid water stress which worsens thrips damage

common_pests_diseases.name: Purple blotch
common_pests_diseases.remedy: Avoid overhead watering, ensure good spacing for airflow

common_pests_diseases.name: Downy mildew
common_pests_diseases.sign: Pale patches turning grey/purple, fuzzy growth
common_pests_diseases.remedy: Improve drainage and airflow, remove infected leaves

harvest_timeline.signs_of_readiness: Leaves/tops yellow, fall over, and begin to dry
harvest_timeline.harvest_method: Loosen soil and lift bulbs by hand, leave to cure (dry) in a shaded, airy spot for 1-2 weeks before storage

beginner_tips: Start from seed in a nursery bed, transplant seedlings after 6-8 weeks when about pencil-thickness
Stop watering as bulbs mature - this helps the neck dry down which improves storage life
Cure (dry) onions properly after harvest in a shaded, ventilated area before storing to prevent rot
